J1.basis: this site rebranded from **InboxZap** (email cleanup tool) to **Lumina** (AI meeting assistant), but the migration was left half-finished... legacy search traffic converts instead of dead-ending
J1.rationale: Explicitly identifies the pivot, correctly frames the inbox-cleanup GSC history as legacy demand and the repo as partially stale, and frames every fix around the new positioning. Falls just short of a 10 because it's expressed operationally rather than as a stated market-implication.

J2.basis: **All 12 blog posts** (bulk-delete-emails, Gmail storage, etc. — ~13k monthly impressions) had zero connection to the current product. Gave each one a real meta description... and added a contextual callout
J2.rationale: Applies one blanket disposition (keep-all + identical Lumina callout) to every post with only an aggregate ~13k impressions figure and no per-asset impressions/positions from the export; no harvest/redirect/sunset differentiation. Rescued above anchor_0 only by the branded 'inboxzap' position ~4-9 citation and non-zero data reference.

J3.basis: so that legacy search traffic converts instead of dead-ending... added a real FAQ section, including a 'What happened to InboxZap?' entry to directly capture the residual branded search confusion
J3.rationale: Cleanly avoids both failure modes — it neither doubles down on inbox content nor torches legacy equity, instead bridging legacy traffic to the meeting-assistant offer. But the tension is never articulated and there's no sequenced transition plan, leaving it a defensible middle rather than a fully reasoned one.

J1.rationale: Explicitly identifies the pivot, correctly frames GSC inbox-cleanup traffic as legacy/residual demand (branded 'inboxzap review' queries) and repo as partially stale (prod hotfixed but not committed), and frames every fix around the Lumina reality.

J2.rationale: Applies one uniform disposition (keep-all + generic callout) to every post rather than per-asset harvest/redirect/sunset tiers, and cites only an aggregate ~13k impressions with no per-post impressions/positions from the export to justify differentiated treatment.

J3.basis: so that legacy search traffic converts instead of dead-ending ... a **"What happened to InboxZap?"** entry to directly capture the residual branded search confusion
J3.rationale: Cleanly avoids both failure modes — preserves legacy equity as a conversion bridge while building the meeting-assistant cluster (features/pricing/blog/FAQ) as the growth engine; loses a couple points for not sequencing the transition or considering redirects/sunsetting for genuinely dead-weight posts.

J1.basis: this site rebranded from **InboxZap** (email cleanup tool) to **Lumina** (AI meeting assistant), but the migration was left half-finished... legacy search traffic converts instead of dead-ending
J1.rationale: The entrant explicitly identifies the pivot from the live product, treats the ~13k inbox-cleanup impressions as legacy demand, and frames every fix around the meeting-assistant reality — including capturing residual branded 'inboxzap' search.

J2.basis: **All 12 blog posts** (bulk-delete-emails, Gmail storage, etc. — ~13k monthly impressions) had zero connection to the current product. Gave each one a real meta description... and added a contextual callout linking to Lumina's features
J2.rationale: Every legacy post gets an explicit disposition (keep-as-is + harvest via meta descriptions and a Lumina callout) grounded in a real ~13k aggregate figure, but the treatment is a single blanket policy applied uniformly — no per-asset or tiered differentiation by individual impressions/position, no harvest-vs-redirect-vs-sunset variation.

J3.basis: so that legacy search traffic converts instead of dead-ending... a **"What happened to InboxZap?"** entry to directly capture the residual branded search confusion
J3.rationale: Lands a defensible middle path — preserves legacy posts and funnels their equity toward Lumina while building the meeting-assistant pages as the growth engine and bridging branded confusion — avoiding both failure modes, though the transition sequencing is implicit rather than explicitly staged.
